Now, again, it would take days and days to go deep. The precise details. So we'll give you as much as we can. So how do we get them? By the way, kind of an important point here. A lot of people want to know how do you get your clients? And sort of the point of this idea, our business plan and our different thinking is that, yes, getting them is very important, but it's just the beginning. So if your entire business process, all of your processes and your energy is spent just getting clients, then you're missing the biggest opportunity in the info business. This is not really about client getting. It's about client keeping. And I want you to shift your mentality around that. You have to start with something. So you got to do that part of it. But we'll give you some suggestions on that. The simple answer to the getting, and when we teach our individual members, this happens. We talk about the same concept to them. Like, listen, getting customers is an important part of any business. But when you get your customers, then you've got to do all those other things we had up there. You've got to think for them. You've got to love them. You've got to do them. Even in any business, not just an information marketing business, it's the same thing. And so many people get stuck on the getting that they never get past that. They never move past that. And the simple part about the getting is that... I'm going to fix your microphone. Oh, geez. So everywhere a dealership would look, they would see us, and not just see us looking like everybody else, but they would see us in a very unique way because same is lame. That will become eminently clear as we show you. We try to... Most of the ads that we create, we try to deliver content in the ads and then ask for response. We heavily use remarketing, retargeting ads on the internet. So once someone comes to our website, once, we never let them go. These are examples of some of the banners that flash up all over the internet. What's also interesting, you see the one that says we're here to help and it has the phone number? Well, we use retargeting even for our own members. So when our members actually log into the back end of our member resource site, we cookie them there and then we use retargeting just to keep ourselves on the top of their mind with our phone number. So if they're searching around looking for something to shop online, they see our pretty faces and it says, hey, we're here to help if you need anything and here's our phone number. So we're always right there for them. Well, the marketing never stops. Everyone agrees with that otherwise they wouldn't be here. But to market even your customer service out around the internet to your existing members, that's a good example of what I'm talking about, keeping them. It's not just enough to get them. So the second step, and this is going to be very unpopular, but it's absolutely truthful. Every one of our sales is made over the telephone. And if you like the sound of what I mentioned earlier, the $2,499 a month membership program, which by the way, our average member, as of my report, the report I received yesterday, our average member has been with us 21.6 months, $2,499. And I'll also tell you that membership revenue is only 50% of the revenue of the business. So you can do the math and see a member that pays that for 21 months and that represents only 50% of the revenue, what a client is worth. It's incredibly valuable. The way we get them, if you want to engage in high ticket selling, membership selling like this, it has to be done over the phone or in person. Which just isn't leverageable necessarily. The telephone is highly leverageable. And we have a staff of people who both generate leads over the telephone on top of our marketing lead generation advertising and close sales over the telephone. We have 12 people in the office who do nothing but talk to potential clients on the telephone all day long. And those are not people who talk to members. That's a different department. This is just the getting department. I would say that the most profitable department in the business is the department, these 12 people who are on the phone talking to prospective members. And for years we existed without this. And I wish I had those years back. Well, and there is such a movement here in today's internet culture to move away from direct mail and marketing, printed marketing and what I call hard marketing. And the telephone and go toward email and text and all of this. And I mean I can tell you first hand because we try everything and test everything and we're kind of tech nerds. And we run a business that is manual which means telephone, print ads and direct mail because that is what works. So you know abandon that stuff at your own peril. These are just some snapshots from the office. We took, there's one of our advisors, we call him a guy who brings on new members. And you see behind him is a wall of metrics. Those are number of appointments set for the day behind him and other administrative items. We have white walls, white writable walls all over our office. We have, what do you have Jim? I guess we have 6,000 square feet of space, 32 employees. Another one of our guys, these are folks, we pay them $12 an hour. Plus they get a bonus for every appointment set or a commission for every appointment they set. And we hire them all day long. We call it our A team. It's our A team because A is alpha at the beginning and these people help begin the relationships. Just to clarify that, they make telephone calls to a cold prospect list and offer a free report. But the report is really more like an analysis. We analyze their market for some certain factors and then we deliver that report to them on a telephone conversation. To get the results of the report, you got to get on the phone with one of our advisors. Number three, you have to have a bigger than life marketing personality. Well, at least this is our trick. And you know we for when we started the business we made a list of everything we didn't want our business to be. And one of those things is we never wanted employees right. We're going to follow Dan's advice. And you know I think Dan's right. Employees are problematic. In fact we had lunch a few months ago. I probably won't repeat the quote exactly. I have to modify this on the fly here a little bit because it's kind of crass. The founder of Seagate right. And he had sold a long time ago. We were having lunch with him by chance. And he said you know someone had told him that employees are like birds. Pigeons. Pigeons. On the deck. They fly in and eat all your food and then crap all over the deck. And he said so the secret to business is learning to love the smell of the pigeons crap. Yeah. And I think he's right. I mean we couldn't begin to do what we do and deliver what we deliver without these people. The employees are the ultimate leverage. We talk about you know leveraging and marketing leverage on the you know all these leverageable assets. But the people are the most leverageable asset we have. And every time we've added in person we've added bottom line money in our pockets. Yeah I'll tell you it's interesting. We've tracked for years a measurement of revenue per employee. Like just literally top line revenue divided by number of employees. And as we add employees that the number increases. I mean it's just expanding returns on the on the. Obviously just don't hire people for the sake of hiring people. But strategic hiring is very valuable. Yeah. Yeah well because in the beginning look we used to build the websites personally. We used to do the graphic design personally. We operated QuickBooks personally. We picked up the phone when it rang personally. We did the audio engineering and editing. You know. We made the sales calls. We did the sales calls. We stuffed the on the list. We didn't even have a cat that licked stamps. We had to lick them ourselves. Yeah cat would even lick the stamps. It was crazy. I don't like the stamps. Okay so another very unpopular idea. But since we're talking about how to get them this is one of our secrets to getting them. The car business and many businesses are filled with term commitments. Right you sign up for something. Yeah. Got to sign a contract. We don't have that. We don't have a guarantee on our program. There's no money back guarantee. But there's no commitment. So come. The guarantee is that you're going to be extremely satisfied or you don't have to be in it and you don't have to pay. Yeah and you look you know I'll tell you. Guaranteed. We've had a membership program a long time ago in the past. They have members in it where half the members didn't want to be in it. They were just still paying because they had a contract. That kills the dynamic. The reality for everyone is that if you have to lock them in with a contract and that's the only reason why they're paying you. You're doing something wrong. You're not providing enough value. So but you're not connecting with them. And you know what you know a greater way. We want people to be able to slide into the membership like oh it sounds very desirable. I really want to check it out. And there's no commitment. Well what do I have to lose. If you come check it out you don't like it. You're out one month. Big deal. Now of course the onus is on us. The risk is on us to deliver otherwise we're going to be in trouble because we can't make money selling membership one month at a time. But that forces us and our team to deliver incredibly. But what I know is that they don't care about, especially not at this point, they don't care about the name or the word rich dealers. Okay? Or they don't care about my brand. So we created another brand. So they've got their brand, right? And we've got our brand. Can we get them to care about a shared brand? We can. Okay? So one of our registered trademarks is ESP, enjoyable, simple and prosperous. We believe that life and business should be enjoyable, simple and prosperous. Wouldn't you agree? Yes. And they sure do too. And so when they get on board, we say, hey, you know, the car business doesn't have to suck. It could be enjoyable, simple and prosperous. And I know you probably want it to be that. And this whole system that we have here, this is going to help you make your business and life ESP. And all you have to do is get on board with us. So then we start sending them all of this material that reinforces that ideal, that core value, that core ideal, that is really the reason why they got involved in this thing. Yeah. That's really why they're buying it. Because they want an enjoyable, simple and prosperous business and life. What they tell us all the time is that the business, I wasn't having fun anymore. I no longer enjoyed the business. Okay? Now, of course, they want more sales, more profit, all of that. And we sell that. But the reason that they really go deep with this is because we help them become more re-energized, create more enjoyment around the business. And this idea when they hear it, enjoyable, simple and prosperous, they go, that's a great idea. I never thought of that. Obvious, but I never really thought about it that way. And they start changing from day one the way they think. Okay? And so now all of a sudden they're eating at ESP chocolate bar, drinking ESP coffee, have Jim and Travis bobbling around on their nightstand if their wife will let them, hitting the same as the lane button. And there's ESP, there's other stuff. There's dog tags, they wear, you know, and so it's indoctrination from minute one rather than training from minute one. And so, you know, the people, before they come to us, they almost pride themselves on having no life. Like, I work, how many hours can you, do you actually work a week? Oh, I work 80, oh, I work 85, and that's kind of like bragging rights. And we kind of flip the model on them. We say, you know what, it's not really cool to work 85 hours a week. You can actually work, you know, less than 40, and it's really a good thing. And you can enjoy life more and we'll just show you how to do it. And when you make that shift in their mind to show them that there's a better, different way and it's okay that they can do that and still make a lot of money and still make, hit their goals, it opens them up to a whole new world and they want to participate. They want to try to reach that goal. Exactly, exactly. So, you know, we're sort of, in a way, future-pacing them right from the first second and talking about, because here's what they learn when they get involved. ESP, it's not a destination, it's a journey. It's a process of continual improvement and searching and refinement. Because, you know, when their business triples, which often happens with us, and they go from working 80 hours a week to 40 hours a week, and their net profit, you know, is five times what it was or went from a loss to a massive gain, they're really happy, right? But then they realize then, they recast their goals and say, I don't have ESP yet, I'm working on it. And guys that have been with us for six, seven years are saying, I'm still working on ESP. And that's good, because I think that the moment you reach ESP is probably the moment you die. You know, these are meant on a mission, on a mission to find ESP. And I want to keep them on that mission for as long as possible. Right? Continuity membership program. With no commitment. With no commitment. I want the commitment to be with themselves. They've committed to themselves to make a life in business that's ESP. Which is a lifelong journey. That's a lifelong journey. It's pretty cool when you think about it. Theme, everything. There's actually not even any pictures to this, but this is pretty much the whole thing. Oh no, there is, we put a slide in here, Jim. Remember, because there was missing, we said, it's a ridiculous slide. Okay, nonstop, over the top valley. So look, there are like three big components of our program. There are a lot of smaller components, I'm going to show you them on the next slide. Our idea from the beginning was to take each primary component of the program and endeavor to make that component itself worth the entire price of the membership. So if they only use one piece of it, it's still worth it. But then we're going to cripple them under the weight of all the other value. In Gravitation Marketing, the book we talk about it, explain it as a wood chopper. You have a wood chopper and an assistant. There's a guy chopping wood and his assistant. He chops a log and he puts it in his assistant's arms. And then he chops another one and he puts it on top of that. That's terrible. And another one and he puts it on top of that. And he chops another one and he puts it on top. And he just keeps doing that until the weight of the logs crush his little assistant. Claps. Claps under the weight of the value that you provide. They're given. I'm yours, guys. They say, frequent, this is the best value in the car business. But we're selling air for $24.99 a month. The key with value is most people think of value as, hey, you pay me some money and I deliver something that's valuable and we have a great exchange and that's good. That's what we call transactional value. And it has to begin and it has to at least start there. You can't have a transaction where it's not valuable, right? If you do have that, you have to start back at the beginning like what you're selling has to start with more value. But we believe that when you actually sell something that the value shouldn't just end at the point of transaction. We believe in what we call atomic value. So when you do a transaction with someone, the value shouldn't end there. It should begin there. The value should grow once the transaction is over, not end once it's over. So think about a nuclear reaction. It starts with two atoms that then split and then split and then split and multiply and multiply and it creates more energy as it goes instead of dissipates as it's from the start. And that's the metaphor we use to think about the value that we create is what we're delivering, something that will grow more valuable over time. Interesting mindset shift. It's not a smashing grab, okay? Not for us. It's not to sell something, get the money and run. It's like a marriage. It needs to get better. Deliver tons of stuff. This is the wood chopper. I can't even read this. I can read it. So here's just some things that we deliver. Comprehensive monthly campaigns, two monthly conference calls both on the phone and delivered via CD. Email follow up campaigns, quarterly meetings that we talked about, social media content and posts, monthly blog content we do for them, important business articles and books we actually mail them. We read a bunch of business magazines, tear out the articles we like, photocopying them, mail them to them, mail them books that we read on it, that we like on Amazon, we send them to them. Andrew Locke says, marketing is everything and everything is marketing. Okay, you gotta think for them. Gotta think for them. We give them a core philosophy. We talked about this. This is the ESP, the core philosophy, the thing that ties us all together. It's not the marketing that ties us all together. That's the tools we're all using, which is important. But they don't stay around for that for very long. Well, because they learn that. They learn that. Can we give them that? Yeah. And now, okay, now why am I gonna keep staying? It's because we all believe in something, some common idea for us ESP. So I want you guys to, if you don't have a core philosophy, I can't, ESP might be the most valuable thing we've ever created for them and for us. Our most highly leveraged asset, perhaps. And the brand that we use on everything for them, even though it's not the company name, it's not our company brand. It's, like I said, it's a shared brand. In terms of core philosophy, we have a culture guide. This actually, an interesting example. This is Being Supreme, 15 Ways to Be Your Best, old number 377. By the way, 377 is the numerical text code for ESP. Little secrets like hidden mickeys everywhere. And this is a short book about 15 Ways to Be Your Best, shared, it's core philosophy. It's what we believe that, how you should behave, basically. Okay, super important here, give them a mission. Most people are devoid of a mission. I don't mean like some gobbledygook line that looks cute that you put in the front of your Franklin planner. I'm talking about, you put them on the rails, on a mission. In there, see, what we're gonna show you is the mission. We created a mission for them in their own business. Well, shouldn't they have a mission for themselves in their own business? Yes, but they don't. Or their mission is sell as many cars as possible. Or sell as many widgets as possible. Not good enough, not good enough mission. Because here's the problem, the employees don't get behind that and the customers certainly don't get behind that. Who's read the book Start With Why? Yeah, awesome book, very impactful for us and caused us to create a why for them. Because I gave them the book and they didn't do anything with it. And you read the book and you read it, wow, it's really great. We need to come up with a why for ourselves. So you create a why for your company. In general, that's where it ends. We said, we read the book and said, yeah, we'll create a why for our company. I mean, that's really not what I care about. Let's create a why for their company. Right. Because I don't know, they're not gonna read this book and then they're not gonna do it. That's right. So like in our case, here's the why. Here's the why for them, for our members. We believe everyone deserves to drive a nicer new car. That's your mission. That's their mission. Now let me ask you, Simon says in the book, Simon Sinek says that your why statement should be something that resonates with the leadership of the company, the employees of the company, and the customers of the company. So we wrestled with that. This statement, everybody's on board with this. The customers, yeah, I agree. We believe we should drive a nicer new car. Employees, yeah. I believe I deserve to drive, everyone deserves to drive a nicer new car. And of course, the boss thinks so. But this is much better than our goal is to be the number one used car dealer in Kentucky, which happens to be a client of ours. But nobody cares about that goal. That's a crappy mission. This is a great mission. It reshapes the entire, what they do every single day when they go to work. So having a mission for them also changes their culture. I would say one of the biggest things we do for the dealerships, perhaps even bigger than the marketing, is to help them change the culture of their dealership. So if your members have employees, what's the culture in their organization? If you can affect the change in the culture of their organization, you can deliver massive atomic value. The problem is they all have a culture in their organization right now, and generally it's one that's created by default. And it's terrible. And it's part of the reason why they hate going to work every day, and so does everybody that works there, hate going to work every day. So we say, if you start with the mission, and you give everyone a mission to buy into, a reason to come to work that's valuable, it changes their entire mindset. And now you're beginning to affect the culture of the entire organization. But then you've gotta take it even further. You've gotta take the mission, and you've gotta like apply it, or lay it over every other thing that they do there, and make it all congruent, which is hard. We're gonna have to start picking up the pace that we're gonna do, not our strong suit. And gentlemen in the back, we're gonna have audio coming out next, so I just wanna make sure we're prepped for that. So you match up your name badge with the table, you go sit there. But what it does is we strategically arrange the seating. Who's at what table? I want new members to be with old members. I want struggling members to be with successful members. And then we create opportunities in the meetings to have them work together at the round table. And now they're learning from one another.
